title: China’s Open AI Models Are Advancing Its Global Soft Power | NOEMA
gist: Gardels argues China is winning global AI diffusion through cheaper, adaptable open-weight models (DeepSeek, GLM 5.2) while America ships expensive closed models—turning open-source into soft power, software-supply-chain leverage, and a spur for nations to secure sovereign AI after US export controls.

insights.claim: A closed society (China) is winning global AI adoption with open-source models while the most open society (America) produces mostly closed, proprietary models that are less adaptable for the rest of the world and far more expensive.
insights.speaker: NATHAN GARDELS
insights.so_what: Frames the central AI-distribution risk for US labs: closed/expensive models ceding global installed base to cheaper open Chinese alternatives.
insights.quote: It is a paradox that a closed society like China is conquering the world with open-source AI models while the most open society, America, is producing mostly closed-source proprietary models

insights.claim: US closed-weight models currently lead Chinese models in raw capability, but China leads the world in open-weight models—so nations wanting an open alternative are adopting Chinese ones.
insights.speaker: Andrew Ng
insights.so_what: Capability lead and distribution lead are diverging; watch whether open-weight adoption locks in ecosystems faster than frontier capability can monetize.
insights.quote: Right now, the U.S.’s closed-weight models are ahead of the Chinese models, but China leads the world in open-weight models.

insights.claim: Publishing open-weight models is a 'brilliant move' by Chinese companies because it rapidly increases the rate of knowledge diffusion within their companies and ecosystem.
insights.speaker: Andrew Ng
insights.so_what: Open-weighting as an ecosystem-acceleration strategy, not charity—relevant to evaluating why Chinese labs give models away.
insights.locator: For Ng, this is a “brilliant move”

insights.claim: Chinese startup Z.ai's frontier GLM 5.2 model is now 'as good as the currently available models from OpenAI and Anthropic,' according to a Trump science-and-tech advisor.
insights.speaker: NATHAN GARDELS
insights.so_what: A US official conceding capability parity on an open Chinese model undercuts the assumption of a durable American frontier moat.
insights.quote: We now have a Chinese open-weight model that is as good as the currently available models from OpenAI and Anthropic.

insights.claim: Across African nations, Chinese models like DeepSeek are adopted far more widely than American models, which 'have really fallen behind.'
insights.speaker: Andrew Ng
insights.so_what: Emerging markets are becoming Chinese-model territory—an installed-base beachhead with long-run soft-power and standards implications.
insights.quote: the Chinese DeepSeek and a handful of other Chinese models are adopted very widely, far more than American models, which have really fallen behind

insights.claim: Whichever model a user queries shapes value-laden answers (e.g., on Tiananmen Square 1989), making global model adoption a powerful channel of soft-power influence comparable to Hollywood storytelling.
insights.speaker: Andrew Ng
insights.so_what: Model provenance embeds political values into everyday answers—an argument for treating AI adoption as a strategic, not neutral, choice.
insights.locator: First, “the storytelling we’ve seen in Hollywood

insights.claim: As more companies build software on a Chinese-dominant AI supply chain, it weakens America's ability to shape how the technology develops.
insights.speaker: Andrew Ng
insights.so_what: Reframes AI leadership as control of the software supply chain, not just model benchmarks—key to long-term standards influence.
insights.quote: AI is a key part of the supply chain of how we build a lot of software products.
insights.locator: Second, “AI is a key part of the supply chain

themes.theme: Export controls backfiring into AI sovereignty

insights.claim: The White House's shutdown of foreign access to Anthropic's Fable 5 model (later lifted after review) demonstrated the US can yank AI access, accelerating urgency in many capitals to secure sovereign AI supply.
insights.speaker: Andrew Ng
insights.so_what: US export-control leverage is pushing nations toward open weights precisely because those cannot be revoked—a self-undermining soft-power dynamic.

insights.claim: Nations are turning to open-weight models specifically because once they possess all the weights, no one can take the technology away from them—an unfortunate net effect on America's soft power.
insights.speaker: Andrew Ng
insights.so_what: Irrevocability is a core selling point of open weights for sovereign buyers—structural tailwind for open-model adoption regardless of frontier gaps.
insights.quote: once they have all the numbers no one can take it away from them

themes.theme: Cultural bias, safety and arms-control framing

insights.claim: LLMs everywhere carry the imprint of cultural-political values—China censors Party criticism, the West censors sensitive race/gender speech, the Islamic world censors blasphemy—so no model is value-neutral.
insights.speaker: Kai-Fu Lee
insights.so_what: Undercuts the 'Western models are objective, Chinese models censored' binary; value-alignment is universal and shapes market fit by region.
insights.quote: Different cultural zones with different values will censor different things.

insights.claim: The adaptability of China's open-source models to local circumstances, along with cost, is the core competitive advantage—it is diversity that drives their diffusion.
insights.speaker: NATHAN GARDELS
insights.so_what: Localizability plus price, not raw benchmarks, is the winning axis in emerging markets—where US closed models are weakest.
insights.quote: it is diversity that drives the diffusion of China’s open-source models

insights.claim: Open-source proliferation is the bigger safety worry because guardrails installed via RLHF must be engineered so bad actors cannot 'back them out' once a model is released.
insights.speaker: Eric Schmidt
insights.so_what: Tamper-resistant safety on open weights is an unsolved technical problem and a likely regulatory pressure point on open-model releases.
insights.quote: It has to not be easy to make open-source models unsafe once they have been made safe.

insights.claim: Schmidt proposes a Cold War-style 'no-surprise rule' requiring the US and China to notify each other before training anything completely new on the AI frontier, echoing nuclear-era transparency.
insights.speaker: Eric Schmidt
insights.so_what: A concrete US-China AI arms-control proposal to watch as a template for any future governance regime.
insights.quote: if you’re going to do training for something that’s completely new on the AI frontier, you have to tell the other side that you’re doing it
insights.locator: “One thing I think both sides should agree on
